data.title: Talen Energy Still Has Plenty Of Value To Unlock (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

Talen Energy Corporation (NASDAQ:TLN) stock price is up over 100% YTD, making it one of the best-performing large-cap stocks so far this year. These gains have stemmed from both a lucrative power supply deal with Amazon as well as the acquisition of two large-scale combined cycle gas turbine (CCGT) power plants.

After such a run, the prevailing question is just how much of the company's future growth is already priced into the stock. With the company on track to generate just $1 billion in 2025 EBITDA, the current valuation is obviously very lofty. However, using a robust industry comparison, I believe TLN is likely to deliver market-beating performance.

In its current form, TLN is fairly valued all the way out to current 2027 earnings expectations. However, the company has multiple growth objectives that can deliver S&P 500 equivalent (or slightly higher) price appreciation. For this reason, I rate TLN as a BUY recommendation.

Update From Previous Coverage

I last covered TLN in May when I downgraded the stock to a hold following the introduction of a price collar on PJM capacity prices. At the time, I was expecting the power market equivalent of a short squeeze. I saw a market with dramatically ramping demand with little too no change in supply. However, following government intervention, this idea did not pan out.

The accuracy of this recommendation was shortly derailed by the announcement of a purchase power agreement with Amazon at the company's co-located data center complex. This was followed by a $3.5 billion acquisition to acquire two CCGT power plants, thereby increasing total capacity by 3 gigawatts.

The two transactions have powered TLN's stock to a 100% gain for the year. Now that the stock has soared past my previous price targets of $250 and $300/share, I believe it is time to see if the company still has what it takes to produce a profit for investors.

New Power Plants And A Strong Capacity Auction Lead The Way

The recent PJM capacity auction results that were released in July, marked the continuation of strong growth in the company's baseline cash flows. Capacity prices for the 2026-2027 calendar year hit the cap at $329/MW-day, a 22% increase over the prior year. This is also up from just $70/MW-day that was awarded in the 2024-2025 auction.

For context, this likely covers at least 90% of the operating expenses of the company's Susquehanna nuclear station. The company expects this to result in $805 million in contracted revenue during the planning year. This alone is over 20% of the company's trailing 12-month revenue. With no additional capital needed to realize this benefit, this translates into pure margin gains for TLN.

Adding to this momentum, are the company's acquisition of two CCGT power plants, capable of a combined 3 GWs worth of power generation. When compared to TLN's older and conventional gas turbines, these two plants demonstrate a vast difference in overall efficiency.

The Moxie and Guernsey plants have a heat rate of just 6.5MMbtu/MWhr, consuming approximately 35% less fuel per MWhr produced than TLN's Brunner, Martins Creek, and Montour plants. This difference in efficiency represents a sizeable opportunity for TLN to capture a higher operating margin and the added bonus of additional capacity-based revenue.

The company originally announced this deal as transacting at 6.7x EBITDA and 40% accretive to FCF/share. This is one of the main drivers in allowing the company to improve its FCF/share projections by over 50% compared to the previous year's investor meeting.

I Still See A Path For TLN To Sustain Capital Appreciation

As noted in the slide below, TLN is projecting a step change in both 2026 EBITDA and FCF growth. This is followed by solid gains in 2027, resulting in EBITDA doubling from today's value. The company has provided minimum projections for 2028, but has also provided several meaningful avenues leading to further upside.

The company believes that there is the potential for 40% upside to 2028 FCF projections. This upside comes from four different buckets with varying degrees of certainty.

From the items listed below, I would classify the 20% allocated to share repurchases and the scaling of the AWS contract as fairly likely to be realized. Striking a new data center PPA deal or acquiring more power plants requires significant legwork to negotiate, but is certainly reasonable given TLN's own track record and peer (VST) ability to construct a PPA at its Comanche Peak facility.

To capture this wide range of potential outcomes, I have taken three different growth scenarios (20%, 30%, and 40%) to determine a potential range of 2028 EBITDA and FCF. I then applied a 10% compound growth rate to TLN's share price to simulate market-level performance through year-end 2028.

If the three growth scenarios still produce a fair valuation, then we can be reasonably sure that TLN can at least keep pace with the general market, and not all of its growth is already priced in. To determine what is "reasonable" for a valuation, I selected the 10-year average EV:EBITDA ratio of comparable utility and IPP stocks to justify how the market tends to value a stable electricity provider.

Determining A Fair 2028 Valuation

The chart below shows the historical average EV/EBITDA ratio for Vistra, Dominion (D), and Duke Energy (DUK). This group has historically traded between 13x and 15x EBITDA.

To give an even more comprehensive data set, I also selected FirstEnergy (FE) and AES Corp. (AES). These two companies have more direct PJM exposure and have historically traded between 10x and 12x due to this region being unregulated.

Using these two data sets, I believe 12.5-13.5x is a fair midpoint to value TLN's future profitability. This was further applied across the three growth scenarios. Using these outcomes, we can see that VST needs to achieve at least a 30% growth rate in 2028 to allow it to beat what would otherwise be market-equivalent performance.

20% 2028 Growth 30% 2028 Growth 40% 2028 Growth
EBITDA $2,500 million $2,700 million $2,900 million
12.5x Multiple $498/share $553/share $584/share
13.5x Multiple $538/share $597/share $631/share
10% CAGR $572/share

In some ways, consistently expecting 25-30% growth in the energy sector seems a bit unrealistic. However, even if TLN comes up a bit short by 2028, the base case of 20% growth can still be aided by the additional growth of the AWS contract in the coming years.

The full scope of this contract leverages the full output of the company's Susquehanna nuclear station, allowing for the full ramp to 1900 MW. The additional margin afforded by the AWS PPA can generate over $5/share of additional FCF above baseline 2027 levels. This essentially amounts to a subsequent 20% improvement and pushes TLN well over the 30% level (although on a slower timeline).

In total, TLN has five growth pillars available between now and the end of this decade. While these five pillars have ranging probabilities of success, it seems likely that 30% growth can be reasonably achieved.

Risks

A significant portion of TLN's FCF growth is attributed to the rise in capacity prices. With the most recent auction results exceeding the cap limit, the table is set for significant gains both in the near and medium terms. This potentially could become even more lucrative once the cap expires.

The 2028/2029 capacity auction will be the first auction without the current cap in effect. As a result, investors are likely bullish on the trajectory of future capacity prices, but these revenues do have strings attached.

PJM awards capacity revenues for being a reliable producer of electricity. However, if a plant trips offline after being bid into the market, that plant must pay back that capacity revenue and is subject to the potential for future penalties. This mechanism is in place to give producers the proper incentive to ensure operators maintain their equipment in a state that keeps the grid reliable.

If TLN does not properly invest in its assets to keep the plants running smoothly, much of the revenue gains could be lost with additional penalties detracting from profitability. This additional investment could impact the company's FCF profile, particularly if any of its stations have a backlog of deferred maintenance.

Investor Key Takeaways

Another strong capacity auction combined with an enhanced PPA with Amazon provides for significant growth in near-term FCF. The acquisition of two CCGT plants significantly increases the size of TLN's operations. These plants are very efficient, allowing the company to improve the margin in the company's fossil business unit.

Following the stock exhibiting large gains this year, much of the near-term growth is adequately priced in. However, the company has five pillars of available growth in 2028. These pillars represent the ability to improve FCF in excess of 40%. Using several different growth scenarios, it remains reasonable that TLN can sustain a 10% CAGR. This allows TLN to remain competitive with the S&P 500 and the broader market. Upside is afforded by scaling of the AWS contract, M&A, and the potential for the company to sign new data center customers to a PPA. I rate TLN as a BUY.

data.title: A Golden Buying Opportunity - 8%+ Yields Every Retiree Should Own
data.description:
When investing to retire on dividends during retirement, one of the best types of stocks to buy is one that pays a high yield that is well covered by underlying cash flows, backed by a defensive business model and a solid balance sheet, and has a potential growth rate that meets or beats inflation for the foreseeable future. Even better is if you can buy the stock after a sharp pullback, allowing you to lock in an attractive valuation on your investment as well as a higher yield than you normally would otherwise. This is how investors can build superior risk-adjusted income streams that enable them to achieve financial independence through dividends while sleeping well at night, knowing that their income is likely to remain stable through thick and thin and grow at a pace that keeps ahead of inflation over time, ultimately delivering strong long-term total returns.

Fortunately, a couple of stocks have pulled back sharply recently that I believe make for highly compelling 8%+ yielding opportunities that every retiree looking to live off dividends should consider owning. This article will detail two of them.

A Near-9% Yield With Growth and Resilience

The first opportunity I am going to talk about is an MLP called MPLX (MPLX). Yes, it does issue a K-1 tax form, but the second stock I will discuss in this article does not and instead issues a 1099. So, investors who prefer to avoid K-1s can skip ahead to the next section. However, for those like me who appreciate the tax advantages that come with K-1s, MPLX is a highly compelling opportunity-especially after its recent 8.5% pullback.

What in particular do I like about MPLX? Well, first of all, it is the kind of investment that you can truly retire on, as it checks all the boxes for reliable passive income.

To start, it offers a forward distribution yield of nearly 9%, while analysts expect it to grow its payout at about a 6.5% CAGR through the end of the decade.

This payout growth appears highly achievable for several reasons. First, management has guided on recent earnings calls that they expect to continue growing the distribution near its recent 12.5% CAGR for the foreseeable future. Additionally, its distribution coverage ratio is quite strong at 1.5x, giving MPLX significant flexibility to continue growing its distribution at a pace that exceeds its underlying cash flow growth.

This is even more encouraging given that its operating cash flow has grown at a 6.9% CAGR since 2021, while adjusted EBITDA has grown at a 6.8% CAGR over the same period-closely aligning with analysts' expectations for distribution-per-unit growth through the end of the decade. Its balance sheet is also in excellent shape, with a leverage ratio of just 3.1x, well below the upper limit of 4x that MPLX targets, giving it substantial flexibility to take on additional leverage when needed to further drive distribution growth.

On top of this, MPLX has one of the most commodity-price-resistant business models in the midstream energy space, thanks to its long-duration, fee-based contracted cash flows. It is also leaning heavily into its natural gas and NGL businesses, with roughly 90% of its growth capital plan currently allocated to these areas. This positions MPLX to benefit from powerful secular trends such as the AI-driven surge in energy demand, the reindustrialization of the United States, and the ongoing NGL export boom.

When you put all these factors together, MPLX stands out as a highly compelling yield-plus-growth opportunity, offering a near-9% distribution yield, strong growth prospects, a fortress balance sheet, and meaningful resistance to both economic downturns and energy price volatility.

Government-Backed 8%+ Yield With Long-Term Stability

Another really attractive opportunity is a triple-net lease REIT called Easterly Government Properties (DEA). Thanks to a recent pullback of nearly 9%, DEA now offers investors a highly compelling 8.3% next-12-month dividend yield while trading at a very attractive valuation.

Management has guided for adjusted funds from operations (AFFO) per share growth of 2-3% annually for the foreseeable future and sees a path to achieving the upper end of that range, especially if interest rates decline somewhat, which would improve its cost of capital and potentially accelerate its growth rate. In the meantime, its dividend is well covered by underlying cash flows, with a payout ratio of roughly 76% on an AFFO basis. Given these very stable cash flows, the dividend appears quite sustainable and is likely to grow in line with AFFO per share over the long term.

The reason DEA's cash flows are so stable is that triple-net lease REITs (VNQ) are inherently defensive, and DEA's portfolio in particular is exceptionally secure. It has a 9.6-year weighted average remaining lease term across its portfolio, which is 97% leased at the moment. Additionally, its tenants are overwhelmingly composed of U.S. government agencies and investment-grade counterparties, providing strong confidence that rent payments will continue uninterrupted regardless of economic conditions.

Furthermore, DEA's properties are mostly mission-critical and built-to-suit facilities that are very difficult and costly to replace, giving it a durable competitive advantage. With its pipeline of new developments having slowed considerably, DEA faces even less risk of disruption than before. Moreover, because its leases are tied to essential government operations, the company's rent collections should not be impacted by any government shutdowns.

When you combine its very dependable cash flow stream with its high current yield and solid growth outlook that should roughly keep pace with inflation, DEA looks like a highly compelling source of passive income for retirees in the current environment.

Risk Analysis and Investor Takeaway

Between MPLX and DEA, investors can lock in 8%-plus dividend yields that should grow at a rate that outpaces inflation for the foreseeable future while also enjoying the solid defensiveness of their business models. Of course, neither is risk-free, as MPLX's cash flows and near-term unit price performance are still somewhat sensitive to energy price volatility, and MPLX also has potential conflicts of interest with its largest unitholder and management team from Marathon Petroleum (MPC). However, so far MPLX's distribution has continued to grow even during the worst of energy price crashes, and MPLX's management team has done a great job of allocating capital, as evidenced by its impressive distribution growth over time and sector-beating (AMLP) total returns:

Meanwhile, DEA's share price and growth potential remain sensitive to long-term interest rates. However, even if long-term interest rates remain higher for longer, DEA's yield is high enough and its retained cash flow is significant enough that it should still deliver a double-digit annualized total return over time alongside its attractive dividend yield. Given their strengths, these are exactly the sorts of opportunities I am loading up on in my portfolios at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The 9 Companies I Trust With My Family's Money
data.description:
Readers have asked me for an update on my family's portfolio, so here it is.

Short, sweet, and I'll provide even better ideas and actionable ideas in the near future.

This is about my favorite real money ideas. What my family is trusting with 100% of our savings.

This is me being transparent about my portfolio, not as personalized investment advice, but as a way to help you see how I apply disciplined, rules-based investment strategies.

A Quick Reminder About Why This Matters To My Family

  • Zen
  • Extraordinary
  • Ultra
  • Sleep Well At Night
  • Long-term
  • Ethical
  • Growth
  • And
  • Charitable
  • Yield

Yes, I really am this nerdy, so let's take a quick look at ZEUS, how it works, and what the plan for 2026 is (as of early October).

  • December is the annual rebalancing.

ZEUS Is Everything To My Family Right Now

It was a busy week in terms of dividend reinvestment top-ups, 5% crashes in MercadoLibre (MELI) and Humana (HUM), and one limit order filling (my good-till-cancelled limits expired that day, and I'll reset them after the December rebalancing).

ZEUS LEGACY Today

Morningstar's DCF estimate for ZEUS is 7% undervalued, implying 7.5% upside to fair value, or $480,000. How cool are valuation boosts! ZEUS could go up 7.5% in a day, and it would be 100% sanctified by the righteousness of fundamentals.

An important lesson about feelings vs. reality

Wow, so many of those year-to-date results are surprises. Just goes to show the importance of stepping back and seeing the big picture.

I feel like AMZN and BAM have been killing it this year.... nope.

I feel like MELI is down a lot... nope.

I feel like CTA has been terrible... but it's up 2%.

Feelings will lie to you. Numbers (if you know where to find reliable ones) won't... if you have the proper context to understand them.

But what about the fundamentals for ZEUS?

20X earnings? Is that a lot? It's 16% less than the S&P, and according to Morningstar's DCF analysis, ZEUS is 8% undervalued.

  • 10% upside potential to fair value.

21.7 PE would be Morningstar's estimate for fair value with a 3.1% yield and 19.5% growth for a 22.6% long-term total return potential at this exact snapshot in time.

  • The 23% CAGR estimate from Morningstar when we started in December 2023, vs. the 23% CAGR realized.
  • "All models are wrong, but some are useful." George E.P. Box

The 44% weighted return on equity is 27% above the S&P's, indicating very strong quality and profitability.

OK, but what about the total return potential of what I have left?

Why I Sold AES (And Emailed DK Members About It) Part 1: Not Much Upside Left

AES (AES) was a recommendation made five months ago when it was trading at 4.7 times earnings, compared to a historical 12.2 times 10-year average.

And then it fell a bit more, bottoming around 4X earnings at its 52-week low.

It was a dirty value utility that used debt-funded growth to try to achieve 8% growth (management guidance).

The end of green energy subsidies (AES had bet everything on growth via renewables) is why AES was disliked by the market.

And bottomed at 4X (10X EV/EBITDA).

And then BlackRock came in with a $38 billion deal (market cap was just $10 billion, due to $28 billion in debt).

While some analysts speculate that there might be a bidding war and BlackRock might have to pay $18, the actual offer was $15.30, and since AES hit that (and slightly surpassed it) on the day of the announcement, I pulled the trigger and cashed out 100% at a 55% profit in five months.

Including the 600 shares of BTI I sold to permit me to buy more Global Payments (GPN) during its quarterly review, which resulted in around $60,000 in total realized gains.

  • About $12K in potential taxes.
  • And donations of $5,400.

Why I rolled 100% of that into MercadoLibre (MELI) The Same Day

MELI is down about 10% on some minor news about Mexico investigating them for antitrust concerns. I just rolled AES proceeds (170K) into 77 shares of MELI.

Here's why. MELI is trading at 20X EV/FCF (the No. 1 valuation metric for accuracy and profitability since 1990). 34% CAGR consensus growth (FactSet consensus from 28 analysts).

That's a PEGY ratio of 0.589 vs a historical 2.17. That's a 73% historical PEGY discount on MELI, the Amazon of Latin America. And here's the growth consensus by year for this 20 EV/FCF company.

20% in 2025, 47% in 2026, 36% in 2027, 39% in 2028, 27% in 2029, and 27% in 2030. So what does this mean?

Return potential wise? 135% total return potential by the end of 2026 and 618% by the end of 2030. 7.2X in five years potential. MELI is now 4.25% of the portfolio, which is the target allocation for 2026—1/6th of the growth bucket for MELI."

With MELI, the question isn't whether money will be made in the next five years, but likely how much.

The trailing PE of 54 might make you think MELI isn't a deep value stock, but it is.

A stock trading at 19X book value!

And 54X earnings!

Yes, but just 20X cash flow.

And 20X EV/FCF, the #1 value metric of the last 33 years.

The gold standard value metric... using the last 13 years median data to get the objective market-determined fair value.

And you get 0.58 PEGY (0.51 PEG measured the traditional way).

By definition, this can't be overvalued.

And especially given that the 13-year median PEGY was 2.17.

If MELI is worth a PEGY of 2.17 (and billions of investors over 13 years buying and selling it said it was), then 0.59 PEGY (or 0.51 PEG) can't be overvalued.

I don't care if the PE is 54, I don't care if the P/B is 19.

The math is 100% clear on this; MELI is undervalued by 73% based on PEGY.

What If You're Wrong? Margin of Safety

MELI, growing at 28% over 13 years, had an EV/FCF of 60.

It's now growing 34%, and the EV/FCF is 20.

There are two things that can go wrong from here.

Either the growth doesn't happen as expected, or the valuation doesn't mean revert.

The risk profile for MELI is the same as it always was (mostly political risk in Latin America).

However, thanks to growing diversification and rising margins, S&P now rates MELI BBB- (no longer a junk bond, indicating it is no longer a speculative company).

In other words, MELI was a lot riskier in 2011, and slower growing. And it traded at 60X EV/FCF.

Now you want to tell me that faster growth (20% faster, 34% vs 28%) and less fundamental risk (as proven by the investment grade credit rating) deserves a valuation one-third as high?

That seems highly implausible, but let's run with it! Let's say that 20X EV/FCF is the new fair value for MELI.

Now what happens? Well, if it grows as expected, instead of achieving 46% CAGR returns over five years (due to a value boost), you get "just" 34%, because the EV/FCF (and PE) remain where they are today.

OK, what if the price doesn't keep up with growth? More multiple compression?

What if the EV/FCF falls to 15? Or 10?

Then the PEG falls from 0.33 to 0.44.

AES traded at 10X EV/FCF at its most hated.

With very few growth prospects.

OK, but what if MELI's growth prospects fade?

What if instead of 34% growth as expected by the 28 analysts who cover it for a living and collectively know it better than anyone other than management?

What if MELI grows at 20% over the next five years?

Then a 20 EV/FCF is a PEGY of 1.

By definition, that's Peter Lynch's GARP, growth at a reasonable price.

At which point you make "only" 20% CAGR over the next five years.

Do you see the power of a 73% PEGY undervalued MELI that's currently growing at 34% CAGR?

The growth markets are enormous, the moat is wide, the optionality is Amazon-like, and the company is trading at a PEGY of 0.59.

Whether you're looking at rules of thumb (PEGY of 1 is GARP) or relative value (0.59 PEGY vs 2.17 13-year median), MELI is so undervalued that even if the growth comes in 50% lower than expected, the PEGY ratio would still be 1.2.

In other words, to lose money on MELI during the next five years would require some kind of global economic catastrophe that devastates the Latin American economy... or multiple compressions that would make one of the deepest coiled springs on Wall Street even more insanely undervalued.

What if MELI trades flat for a few years? If it grows as expected, the EV/FCF would fall to 7.4 by 2028, and its PEGY would be 0.21, compared to a historical 2.17.

If the price stayed flat for five years? Until 2030? The PE would fall to 10.9 with a PEG of 0.34 vs 1.28 historical.

Do you see what I mean?

It's not a question of making money or losing money.

Barring a downturn in Latin America's economy, the only question is how much investors are likely to earn.

MELI is both a hyper-growth blue chip and a deep-value blue chip.

It's one of the fattest pitches on Wall Street.

And what if it trades flat in 2026? Then it will get cheaper, and I'll buy more.

And if it trades flat for five years? Like how BTI was flat for five years despite steady growth?

Then one day, MELI will return to its fair value. As long as the fundamentals remain intact, mean reversion is a real phenomenon. I can't tell you the timing, but I can tell you that those who patiently buy quality at a great price are eventually rewarded.

PM is the most successful recommendation I've made in nine years on Seeking Alpha, averaging 77% total returns (including dividends) in the first 12 months.

Not because PM was red hot, but because when it became red hot, the returns were spectacular.

BTI was terrible for five years... I recommended it 89 times.

And then, boom, it's the new hotness.

Prophecy fulfilled.

I can't tell you when a blue chip that isn't broken will return to historical fair value, but unless the thesis does break, it eventually does.

Buffett-like returns from blue-chip bargains hiding in plain sight aren't just a funny phrase; it's the truth.

  • A good number of my recommendations since 2016 have delivered Buffett-like stock price returns (excluding dividends) in the first 12 months.
  • Including dividends, the numbers are better.

I don't care about 12-month returns. However, I deliver on them because all I do is point out that if a blue chip is trading at a PEGY below its historical norm, unless its fundamentals continue to deteriorate, the odds are very high for excellent returns.

It's this same strategy that allowed our model portfolios to do so well.

20% annual returns on a high-yield portfolio for six years... not magic, just math.

I'm not a genius, I'm a disciplined investor who has learned a few things.

  1. All models are wrong, but some are useful. (You've never perfected a model.)
  2. Market-determined fair value is best (value vs. historical value).
  3. The right metrics matter (MELI is a screaming bargain unless you only look at PE and ignore growth rates or think book value has anything to do with modern non-financial valuations).

Throw in the right safety and quality model to ensure that the thesis isn't compromised, and implement disciplined risk management (with risk caps on every position based on quality). Anyone in the world could then replicate what I've done.

I'm nothing more than a rules-based nerd who is good at drawing lines on charts and knows how to drill down to the 1st principles truth, ignoring stories, politics, and all the noise that makes investors outsmart themselves.

Is there a lot of luck involved in this kind of success? Yes, and no.

Luck is what happens when preparation meets opportunity." Seneca

"This luck is sanctified by the righteousness of fundamentals" Adam Galas

Coming soon is a full review of all my ZEUS holdings and the plans for 2026, including why my family is so excited about our future.

I'm so excited to share it with you!

data.title: CGDV: Does It Now Belong Among The Dividend ETF Elite?
data.description:
If you read anything around the dividend ETF space, it probably centers around one of these four funds:

  • Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG).
  • Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM).
  • Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).
  • iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO).

These are the largest dividend ETFs in the marketplace and among the cheapest options you'll find anywhere.

While there's nothing inherently wrong with any of these dividend ETFs (although I'd argue that VYM's strategy isn't the best way to target high yield), there's one relative newcomer that's been muscling its way into the conversation.

The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (NYSEARCA:CGDV) launched in February 2022. Since then, it's only grown to nearly $23 billion in AUM and is now the 5th largest dividend ETF in the marketplace, only behind the four ETFs listed above.

It's a remarkable success that's even more remarkable given how long it took Capital Group to get involved in the ETF space to begin with.

Most investors are familiar with Capital Group's mutual fund arm, the American Funds. Those funds have been around for decades and have been immensely popular with both retail investors and financial advisors.

For a long time, Capital Group resisted entering the ETF until it finally did so in 2022 with the launch of CGDV and five other core-style ETFs. The big differentiator was that Capital Group opted to go with actively managed strategies. It wasn't going to compete with Vanguard and BlackRock on cost. It was going to compete on research expertise and brand recognition.

So far, it's been an incredibly successful venture. Capital Group has grown to become the 11th largest ETF issuer with AUM of more than $93 billion.

CGDV is one of the main catalysts. It's become the largest ETF in the Capital Group lineup, and its strong performance record is a big reason why.

What Is The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF?

As mentioned, CGDV is actively managed and its objective is to produce income exceeding the average yield on U.S. stocks and to provide an opportunity for growth of principal consistent with sound common stock investing. It does this by investing primarily in dividend-paying stocks of larger established U.S. companies.

The "primarily" part needs clarifying. On CGDV's fund page, it uses the phrase "value refined" and says that the fund will focus on "companies that pay dividends or have the potential to pay dividends." That means that CGDV, a dividend ETF, can include non-dividend payers in its portfolio.

If you're a dividend purist, you might say this is a bit disingenuous. I've said as much in the past myself when the fund held Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG), and Meta Platforms (META). At the time, none of them were dividend payers. Alphabet and Meta have since initiated dividends, but Amazon still has not. Among other non-dividend-paying holdings - Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX) and Uber (UBER).

Whether I like it or not, it's part of CGDV's strategy. Perhaps the ability to establish a position before they become dividend payers is advantageous, or maybe it's a way to sneak some growthier names into the portfolio. Either way, CGDV has developed quite a track record. It owns a 5-star rating from Morningstar in its Large Blend category, which is not an easy feat.

And it's been an elite performer within the dividend ETF universe.

What Does CGDV's Portfolio Look Like?

It's probably not surprising that CGDV has tech as its top sector holding, but it's not as outsized as some might think.

CGDV's tech weighting is in the top quintile when comparing it within the U.S. dividend ETF universe (source: ETF Action), but it is also very underweight relative to the S&P 500. That suggests a portfolio that's not drifting too far away from its core dividend competency.

The remainder of the portfolio is well diversified. Industrials and healthcare are often among the top sector holdings of dividend ETFs, and that's what we see here as well. Those two sectors, along with consumer discretionary, are noticeably overweight relative to the broad dividend stock space.

The biggest deviation from the average is the financial sector exposure. In many dividend ETFs, that allocation runs around 20%, but it's only 7% in CGDV. Clearly, the management team feels there's not much value to be had here.

The fund's top holdings tell us a few things about how CGDV's management team approaches portfolio construction.

CGDV has a 24% allocation to tech, but 17% of that comes from just three stocks - Microsoft (MSFT), NVIDIA (NVDA), and Broadcom (AVGO). Meta is also there at 2.9%, but that's really a tech-adjacent holding.

The common factor is that all of these stocks are minimal dividend payers. All four have dividend yields of under 1%. NVIDIA's yield is a hilarious 0.02%.

The fact that these companies pay a dividend at all qualifies them for CGDV's portfolio, but dividend history, yield, and growth rate seem to be only minor considerations when building the portfolio. That might make CGDV more of a growth ETF than a dividend ETF.

The same could be said for VIG. It requires a 10-year dividend growth history to qualify, but then weights the portfolio by market cap. That's how you get Microsoft (MSFT) and Apple (AAPL) as that fund's top two holdings, even though their yields are miniscule.

Is CGDV Worth Investing In?

As a long-term core portfolio holding, I think the answer is yes. I think there's an adequate mix of growth, defensive, and cyclical sector exposures here. The way that the portfolio appears to be getting allocated makes it modestly more aggressive than the typical dividend ETF, although it has a better growth/value balance.

The fact that the fund owns a 5-star rating in the Large Blend category (where the Mag 7-heavy S&P 500 ETFs also reside) demonstrates the management team's ability to keep up with the major indices despite an overall strategy that runs more defensive.

CGDV's 1.4% yield isn't going to wow anyone seeking income, but the total return picture is what's more important.

Final Thoughts

I'm having some trouble calling CGDV a dividend ETF at all. Sure, it's mostly dividend stocks, but the fact that it's including some clearly growth-oriented non-dividend payers in the portfolio makes it feel like it's not a fair fight.

Its performance history within the dividend ETF category would suggest it's one of the elites. Its $23 billion AUM size would also qualify it for inclusion among the heavyweights.

But is it a dividend ETF in the truest sense of the word? I have some questions.

data.title: Dell Technologies: A Dinosaur That's Learning New Tricks
data.description:
Perhaps you, like me, have a certain bias against Dell Technologies (NYSE:DELL). Before, when I looked at it from afar, Dell seemed to me to be practically a dinosaur in the tech sector, not because it was obsolete or anything like that, but because it was a much less sexy stock, with more moderate prospects, an old and extremely consolidated company, timid growth, and so on.

But upon further examination, I was pleasantly surprised. Growth is very strong due to AI shipments and has remained accelerated for a couple of quarters, meaning that even if it is a 'dinosaur' (also in the positive sense of the term), the company is learning new tricks.

Not only has growth been very interesting in recent quarters, but the stock continues to trade at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 15x, meaning it combines AI, growth, and value.

The New Trick: Powering the AI Revolution

The main pillar of Dell's thesis currently lies in the Infrastructure Solutions Group. Historically, it has not accounted for the largest share of Dell's revenue, but the outlook for fiscal year 2026 is to substantially exceed the core Client Solutions Group (or CSG) revenue.

Looking at the chart below, the reason is clear. This segment will be boosted by $20 billion in revenue due to AI Server Shipments.

Dell has managed to completely transform this revenue with these AI shipments. More specifically, server and networking revenue grew 69% YoY in Q2, from $7.7 billion to $12.9 billion.

As the right side of the image shows, this is not a coincidence. Dell is well positioned to meet this demand, which is likely to continue at least in the medium term. The company is a leader in innovation and is continuing with initiatives to maintain its leadership and increase its margin in this segment. A clear sign of this ability to adapt is that at the beginning of 2024, there was no revenue from AI server shipments, meaning that much of the current value generation is very recent.

Another point worth mentioning is that the company partners with major industry players, such as Nvidia (NVDA), to deliver high-quality servers that support Nvidia's new GPU architectures.

The backlog is not huge enough to allow us to envision contracted growth for the coming quarters, but it does show that momentum remains strong, and this, together with its positioning in the industry, leads me to believe that if the trend of massive investments continues, Dell will continue to see its financials increase significantly.

Even though it is much less exciting than Nvidia or Palantir (PLTR), Dell is also at the center of this AI race and is benefiting from the huge CapEx that companies are making.

Not That Exciting, but a PC Behemoth

The other big part of Dell, CSG, is not as exciting as the AI trend. It is a segment that is growing very slowly, close to 1% sales CAGR. But still, it is an interesting foundation that adds stability to cash flow and removes dependence on the AI trend.

In this segment, the company is not 'just another' competing with several players but rather ranks number one in several subsegments, such as workstations, NA commercial PCs, and monitors, and has been gaining market share in recent years.

Even though this is not a compelling segment, it is still possible to see some trends, such as the PC update cycle for companies with new technologies emerging and a potential impact of new AI PCs on this.

Risks Are Real, but Also Manageable

The main point about the risks in the Dell Technologies case, in my view, is that the company is still a hardware company. This industry is naturally cyclical. On the one hand, there is a technological leap that drives the update cycle and also causes the world's leading companies to invest in data centers, increasing demand for Dell products. On the other hand, this implies that the industry will eventually slow down. In other words, this double-digit growth will not last forever.

Furthermore, it is a low-margin business. The company's gross margin is 21%, and its net income margin is less than 5%. This makes cyclicality even more concerning, since if something like a decline in revenue occurs, along with an increase in costs, it could be disastrous for cash flow.

Finally, it is worth mentioning competition, since although the company is very well established and positioned, competition is still fierce in this landscape, so the company needs to keep innovating and investing in this area so as not to lose market share eventually.

Valuation: Too Much Growth for This Low P/E

In Dell's long-term goal, the company envisions 15%+ diluted EPS growth per year, with 100% conversion to adjusted free cash flow. As if that weren't enough, the company also mentions that 80% of this should be returned to shareholders. These are all excellent characteristics of compounding.

A solid company, exposed to strong trends, which should grow at a rapid pace and still make buybacks and distribute dividends.

But even with all these positives, the company is still trading at a very attractive valuation. Compared to peers such as Hewlett Packard (HPE), Helmerich & Payne (HP), Cisco (CSCO), and International Business Machines (IBM), its forward GAAP P/E is the lowest among most of them, often second only to HPE, as can be seen in the table below.

In addition to all these multiples that should allow for compounding in the coming years, the company accumulates interesting grades when we look at the whole picture. The valuation is reasonable, growth is good, profitability is good, and EPS revisions are also good.

The Bottom Line

In a landscape where there is much discussion of an AI bubble, of companies that deliver quick returns given the dynamic nature of contracts and increasingly high-growth prospects, it seems interesting to me to have a player who stands out for being more 'down to earth.'

Of course, if the secular AI trend is broken, this affects Dell's thesis, but with already low multiples, very solid cash flow, and a very well-established brand, this makes the downside risk much lower than other more 'sexy' companies.

Therefore, I assign a buy rating to DELL stock.

data.title: China Takes Dramatic Measures, While Japan's MOF Warns Against Excessive Yen Moves
data.description:
Overview

The US dollar (DXY) has stabilized after yesterday's surge. Following words of caution by Japan's finance minister, the yen is the strongest of the G10 currencies today, with around a 0.15% gain. The slowest underlying inflation in Norway in four months has weighed on the krone, which is off about 0.35%. Excluding the krone, the G10 currencies are little changed. Emerging market currencies are in a wide range but also mixed. Since the end of this long national holiday, China has announced several measures, including broader export controls on rare earths, EV battery technology and a levy on US ships making port calls in China.

Japanese and Chinese equities fell, with the major indices off more than 1%. South Korea and Indian markets rallied, but most of the other large bourses in the region fell. Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is little changed, and US index futures are slightly firmer. Benchmark 10-year yields are softer in Japan and Europe, mostly 1-2 bp lower. UK gilts and French bonds are the best performers so far today. French President Macron is expected to name a new prime minister today, and Belgium's credit may be downgraded today. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) is off nearly three basis points to 4.11%. The low for the week is closer to 4.09%. Gold held yesterday's low slightly below $3945 and has reemerged above $4000 today. The record high set Wednesday was almost $4060. November WTI was turned back after approaching $63 on Wednesday and Thursday and has tested the $61 area today. The week's low is near $60.70.

USD: The Dollar Index jumped to almost 99.60 yesterday, its best level since August 1, when it reached 100.25. There appears little on the charts to prevent a return that high, which is important from a technical perspective. It could be the neckline of a double bottom, which, if exceeded, could project a year-end rally toward the late March high near 104.70. That is not our base case. DXY is consolidating in a narrow range so far today, between 99.20 and a little more than 99.40. The federal government remains closed, and the market is pricing in high confidence of a rate cut later this month and about an 80% chance of another cut at the last meeting of the year in December. With no government data, the preliminary October University of Michigan is the data point of the day. Minor slippage is expected in sentiment and expectations. The one- and 5-10-year inflation projected to be stable at 4.7% and 3.7%, respectively. Assuming the government remains shut next week, the Fed's Beige Book and the NY and Philadelphia Fed's surveys are the highlights.

EURO: With the apparent assistance of option-related and stop-loss sales, the euro was driven almost $1.1540 yesterday, the lowest level since August 5. The element of caution comes from the euro's settlement below the lower Bollinger Band (~$1.1575 today). Near-term potential may extend toward the $1.1515-20 area, but the topping pattern may project toward the August 1 low, a little below $1.14. The euro has fallen for four consecutive sessions. It has not fallen every day in a week since the last week of January. The euro has held above $1.1555 today but has been unable to resurface above $1.16, where options for almost 1.9 bln euros expire today. Italy reported a dramatic 2.4% drop in August industrial output. It follows a 0.1% decline in Spain, a 0.7% fall in Spain, and a 4.3% collapse in Germany. The aggregate eurozone figure is due in the middle of next week. Meanwhile, as the French bond market calms, Moody's is set to announce the conclusion of its review of Belgium's credit. The As3 rating (with a negative outlook) is above Fitch's assessment (A+) but below S&P (AA+).

CNY: The dollar stalled on Wednesday in front of CNH7.1545, the (50%) retracement of its losses since August 1. It pulled back yesterday as mainland markets reopened. The greenback fell to a five-day low near CNH7.1240, which was slightly below the 20-day moving average (~CNH7.1285). However, the broad strength of the dollar saw it recover to almost CNH7.14. That held today, and the US dollar has slipped back below CNH7.13. In a firm US dollar environment, the yuan tends to do well. In July, when the US dollar advanced for the first month of the year, the yuan was the strongest currency. During this week's dollar bounce, the offshore yuan is the strongest currency in Asia. The PBOC set the midpoint of the dollar's reference rate at CNY7.1048 today (CNY7.1102 yesterday). China has taken three initiatives over the past 48 hours that are significant. First, it has tightened and extended export controls on rare earths and related technologies, doing in effect what the US has done with semiconductor chips/technology, including extraterritorial application of third-party sales. If enforced, China can demonstrate escalation domination in the semiconductor and AI space as processed rare earths and associated magnets are essential (effective December 1). Second, it announced export restrictions on the equipment necessary to manufacture batteries for EVs (effective November 8). Third, it will charge a special levy for US ships docking in its ports, as the US has done to Chinese vessels (effective October 14). On one hand, this is a significant escalation, but on the other hand, it could be chits to negotiate when Xi and Trump meet later this month on the sidelines of the APEC meeting in South Korea. The 4th plenary session of the 20th Central Committee in Beijing October 20-23.

JPY: Takaichi's election has a new LDP leader and most likely the next Japanese prime minister has injected a new dynamic into the exchange rate. Indeed, in recent days, the exchange rate has been less sensitive to the movement of the US 10-year yield. The rolling 30-day correlation between changes in the exchange rate and the 10-year US Treasury yield has fallen to near 0.40, the lowest in three months. Some observers argue that the yen's weakness adds to pressure on the BOJ to raise rates, but the swaps market sees it differently. At the end of last week, there were 14 bp of tightening discounted for this month's BOJ meeting. Now there are a little more than four. There were 18.5 bp of tightening discounted by year-end, and now about 14 bp. There are two developments to note in Japan today. First, after dramatic yen moves this week, officials have responded with a bout of verbal intervention, as Finance Minister Kato warned that the government is carefully assessing "excessive or disorderly movement." Fear of intervention seems to be still at a low ebb, but it may increase if the dollar approaches JPY155.50, not because the level itself is important but because it represents a 10-yen advance from the low in mid-September. If intervention can be depicted as an escalation ladder, today's verbal intervention is a low rung. Second, the quarter-century coalition between the LDP and the Komeito Party has collapsed, ostensibly over campaign funding reform. Meanwhile, in the US, the president wants the Fed to cut more aggressively, and in Japan, the next prime minister wants the BOJ not to raise rates. The dollar settled above its upper Bollinger Band (two standard deviations above the 20-day moving average) for the fourth consecutive session yesterday. It is found slightly above JPY153.00 today. The dollar recorded a marginal new high today, near JPY153.25, before being sold to about JPY152.40. It is consolidating in the European morning.

GBP: Sterling was sold through the September lows yesterday to $1.3280. The break of September lows in the $1.3325-35 area inflicts potentially serious technical damage. The September lows could be the neckline of a topping pattern that projects losses that may extend toward $1.2945, the (50%) retracement of this year's gains. However, ahead of it is the August 1 low near $1.3140. It has been a dramatic move, and sterling settled below its lower Bollinger Band, which is near $1.3280 today. Sterling has struggled to find traction today. It has held the $1.3280 low but has not been above $1.3315. This week's loss of nearly 1.4% is sterling's largest weekly decline since early January.

CAD: The US dollar is firm and reached a six-month high yesterday of almost CAD1.4035. It settled above the 200-day moving average (CAD1.3980) and CAD1.4000 for the first time since mid-April. There is little to hang one's hat on until closer to CAD1.4150-65. It is consolidating in a narrow range below yesterday's high and above CAD1.4000. Canada reports September employment figures today. Through August, the labor market has slowed. Let's run the numbers. Canada has created about 37.5k jobs in the first eight months compared with 210.5k in the same period last year. It has lost a little less than 1k full-time positions this year and had gained about 86.5k in the year-ago period. The unemployment rate was 6.7% in August 2024 and 7.1% in August 2025. The participation rate has eased to 65.1% in August from 65.4% last August. Wage growth for permanent employees has slowed to 3.59% year-over-year from 4.89% in August 2024. The swaps market goes into the report with about a 55% chance of a cut at the month-end Bank of Canada meeting.

AUD: The Australian dollar posted a bearish outside down day yesterday by trading on both sides of Wednesday's range and settling below its low. Initially, the Aussie recorded the session high in early North American trading yesterday slightly above $0.6610, where it met a buzzsaw of sellers that drove it to a new session low near $0.6540. Wednesday's low was closer to $0.6555. It has been confined to about a quarter-of-a-cent range below $0.6575 so far today. Options for almost A$660 mln at $0.6545 expire today. The next chart support area is in the $0.6500-20 area. A break could signal another cent decline. After poking a little above $0.5800 yesterday, the New Zealand dollar was sold aggressively and fell through Wednesday's low slightly below $0.5740. The $0.5725 area corresponds to the (61.8%) retracement of this year's rally. It is in less than a 20-tick range above $0.5740 today.

MXN: The dollar edged to a new six-session low yesterday near MXN18.30 before its broad recovery pushed it above MXN18.41. It made a marginal new high near MXN18.4165 today before steadying. A trendline drawn off the late September high and last week's and this week's high is found near MXN18.45 today. It falls to about MXN18.4000 at the end of next week. Mexico is expected to report that industrial production stabilized in August after contracting by 1.2% in July. It was the second consecutive monthly decline and left industrial output 2.7% lower than a year ago. The minutes from the recent central bank meeting released yesterday showed most of the board are concerned about economic weakness, though yesterday's inflation showed further upward pressure on prices.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Comcast And Its Real Value
data.description:
Comcast Corporation (NASDAQ:CMCSA) is among the largest players in media and telecommunications and is now reaching a new level of focused development and smarter operations. Following the Versant Media Group (VSNT) spin off, which owns conventional cable networks and certain digital channels, Comcast is redoubling efforts on more rapidly expanding aspects of its business, such as streaming (Peacock), broadband (Xfinity), and theme parks by means of NBCUniversal and Sky. Peacock alone has reached around 41 million subscribers, showing how quickly Comcast's digital side is expanding.

Due to the current changes in the economy, such as lower interest rates and potential regulatory changes, analysts are rethinking Comcast's current position. These shifts could help Comcast to strengthen its cash flow and cut borrowing costs. Right now, the company continues to post good results and has generated $4.5 billion in free cash flow in the second quarter and $5.42 billion in the first. It has also steadily reduced its debt while spending less on capital projects.

Even with the rising competition from fiber and wireless internet providers, Comcast's mix of business and strong financial management allows it to build a solid foundation for long-term growth. One of the biggest things driving the appeal of CMCSA is steady earnings growth and capital returns. The company sustains and grows by pouring money into its most promising areas while rewarding its investors along the way. Comcast is set to be an underrated player in the media and telecom world.

Snapshot of the Company

CMCSA scores 70.5 in terms of company rating out of 100. It appears to have slightly better than average fundamentals, but some categories could use improvement.

Fundamentals

In the moving average chart below, we can see that the price per share has been a mix of steady growth and short-term volatility. From 2016 to 2019, the stock price rose as investors had confidence in broadband growth, cable profits, and NBC Universal's performance. It stayed relatively stable during the pandemic as demand for home internet and streaming increased, covering up the decline in advertising and theme park revenues.

In 2022, there was a sudden surge in price, and the stock peaked at an average of $50.81. This was driven by strong cash flows and optimism around streaming and broadband segments. Unfortunately, this was the same year with rising interest rates, slowed-down ad sales, and an overall decline in the tech sector. It caused the price to pull back sharply, and by 2023, the CMCSA stock averaged $38.15. At this time, the company was dealing with declining cable TV subscriptions as more customers shifted to streaming services. Moreover, there was increased competition in broadband as fiber and fixed wireless providers began to increase.

Comcast managed to regain the trust of the investors with stabilized cash flows, and the stock price recovered in a year and is now on an upward trend. This rebound shows us that the company has disciplined spending, steady cash generation, and a clear focus on growth areas. In general, the average increase in the share price has been 70.84% in the last 10 years, or a compound annual growth rate of 6.13%. Although this is a moderate growth rate in comparison with tech players, it points to the stability and predictability of the company in value creation over the long term. With the industry shifting and the market becoming unstable, the consistent upward trend indicates that Comcast has the capacity to sustain good fundamentals and ensure that it makes good cash flows.

Earnings

Over the past 10 years, Comcast's earnings history shows periods of both strong growth and temporary setbacks. EPS rose from $1.62 in 2015 to a high of $4.75 in 2017, and after that, there were some moderate fluctuations before a sharp dip to $1.21 in 2022. This year, there were some huge industry challenges, high expenses, and weak revenues. Since then, however, the earnings have rebounded sharply to $3.71 in 2023 and $4.14 in 2024. It was due to cost control, good performance of broadband, and contributions from Peacock and NBCUniversal. Even though it isn't a smooth upward trend, the recovery is something notable and should be considered to gauge the company's ability to adapt in the changing media and telecommunication environment.

Return on Equity

The ROE has been fluctuating in the last five years as the profitability and the allocation of capital have changed. The apparent dip is in 2022, when the ROE was 6.07%, which was a year of poor performance in terms of low revenues and high expenditures. But there was a strong rebound of 18.81% in 2023. The five-year average ROE is 14.30%. For return on equity (ROE), I look for a 5-year average of 16% or more, and this is slightly below the preferred benchmark. However, the recent upward trend suggests renewed financial strength and better capital utilization.

Let's compare the ROE of this company to its industry. The average ROE of 9 cable TV companies is 15.50%.

Therefore, CMCSA's 5-year average of 14.29% is slightly below the average, but the current ROE of 19.25% is above the industry average.

Return on Invested Capital

The return on invested capital has similar fluctuations to ROE in the past five years. Some volatility is visible, but so is steady improvement over the years. ROIC, too, dropped in 2022 and hit 4.48%, but recovered in the same year and is now at 10.81%. For return on invested capital (ROIC), I also look for a 5-year average of 16% or more. The 5-year average ROIC for CMCSA is 8.28%, which is far below the preferred benchmark.

Gross Margin Percent

The gross margin percent (GMP) for CMCSA has mostly increased over the last five years. It declined slightly in 2020 but otherwise showed steady growth from 2021 to 2024. Even with the small dip, the overall GMP remains strong. The five-year GMP is solid at 68.67%. I typically look for companies with a gross margin percent consistently above 30%. So CMCSA has shown the ability to maintain very healthy margins over a long period.

Financial Stability

Looking at CMCSA's fundamentals, the company has no long-term debt, which is a very positive sign. This indicates that CMCSA owns more than it owes and is not heavily dependent on borrowing funds for its operations.

Comcast's most recent current ratio was 0.91. Ideally, we'd like to see a current ratio of more than 1, so this area could use slight improvement.

CMCSA offers a dividend yield of 4.25%, which means it returns a healthy portion of profit to shareholders. Overall, the balance sheet suggests that the company is in strong financial health with a good dividend policy.

An analysis of the value of the company vs. the share price would help us gain more insight into where the business stands right now.

Value vs. Price

The estimated value of the stock is $41.33, versus the current stock price of $30.77. This indicates that CMCSA is currently selling below its fair value.

This analysis shows an average valuation of around $49 per share versus its current price of about $31; this would indicate that Comcast is undervalued.

Summarizing the Fundamentals

According to the facts and figures, Comcast Corporation is financially healthy in the long term, with no long-term debt and strong capital management. This indicates that the company owns more than it owes.

Even though the current ratio could use slight improvement, it still has a high level of cash flow in the short term, and it has demonstrated the capability of sustaining operations and investing in high-growth potential areas, as well as rewarding its shareholders.

The company has a good history of earnings, where it has recovered significantly following short-term setbacks. The EPS has been fluctuating within the last decade but has since regained momentum. It shows Comcast's ability to adapt to challenges in the ever-growing and transitioning media and telecommunications industry.

The five-year average of ROE and ROIC is below ideal benchmarks. However, recent trends show impressive improvement. One can say that the company has re-energized its financial resources and made good use of capital. The gross margins have been consistently high over the past years, and this demonstrates that Comcast can be able to sustain profitability in the long run.

Comcast's business model is diversified; it includes broadband and streaming with Peacock, NBCUniversal, and Sky. This is one of the reasons why the company is positioned well for future growth despite the increasing competition from fiber and wireless providers. By focusing on fast-growing segments while controlling costs, the company is building a solid foundation for long-term expansion.

In terms of valuation, my analysis shows that the stock is undervalued.

CMCSA vs. the S&P 500

The chart below shows how Comcast has performed against the market benchmark over the past 10 years. We can see that VFIAX, which represents the general market, has significantly outperformed CMCSA over the last decade. Comcast's stock performance has been low compared to the broader market and its industry in recent years. This downward trend since 2022 is worrisome. On one hand, it could be a visualization of Comcast's slowing growth. On the other hand, some contrarian investors could see this as an opportunity to buy when the stock has been beaten down with the hopes of capitalizing on gains when the stock climbs up closer to its real value.

Forward-Looking Conclusion

Over the next five years, the analysts who follow this company are expecting it to grow earnings at an average annual rate of 3.84%.

In addition, the average one-year price target for this stock is $39.09, which is about a 27.5% increase in a year.

The expected annual compounding rate of return is 14.76%.

Does CMCSA Pass My Checklist?

  1. Company Rating 70+ out of 100? Yes (70.5)
  2. Share Price Compound Annual Growth Rate > 12%? No (6.13%)
  3. Earnings history mostly increasing? Yes (when removing the COVID effect)
  4. ROE (5-year average 16% or greater)? No (14.3%)
  5. ROIC (5-year average 16% or greater)? No (8.28%)
  6. Gross Margin % (5-year average > 30%)? Yes (68.67%)
  7. Debt-to-Equity (less than 1)? Yes (no long-term debt)
  8. Current Ratio (greater than 1)? No (0.90)
  9. Outperformed the S&P 500 during most of the past 10 years? No
  10. Do I think this company will continue to successfully sell their same main product/service for the next 10 years? Maybe the media and telecommunications industry is currently going through many transitions.

CMCSA scored 4.5/10, or 45%. While it doesn't meet all the benchmarks, it is still a financially stable company with strong gross margins and no long-term debt. It shows promise and has been making improvements. Therefore, if conditions continue to improve, it could eventually become a good potential investment again.

Is CMCSA Currently Selling at a Bargain Price?

  1. Price earnings less than 16? Yes (6.92)
  2. Estimated Value greater than Current Stock Price? Yes (value $41.33 > $30.77 stock price)

I feel that Comcast is a solid company with above-average fundamentals. It seems to be trading at a bargain price, and this could be a decent time for some investors to buy with hopes that the company will soon regain losses. In the meantime, they could collect a handsome dividend.

However, I'm not interested in investing at this time. Some fundamentals could use improvement (ROE, ROIC, current ratio, earnings growth). In addition, Comcast's stock has been on a downward trend since 2022, and it fails to outperform the S&P 500 long-term.

At this time, my money seems better allocated to other individual stocks that have better fundamentals, which are also selling at a bargain price. I will keep Comcast on my watchlist since it is showing improvements. Hopefully, once more improvements take place, the stock price will follow.

data.title: ASML: Numerous Reasons To Buy Before Q3 Earnings
data.description:
ASML Holding N.V. (NASDAQ:ASML) is set to release its Q3 earnings next week, and I think there are numerous reasons to buy before earnings. This lithography market's dominator is currently traded extremely cheaply, with multiples at low very levels compared to historical averages. Meanwhile, the AI revolution is not showing signs of cooling down, which will highly likely mean strong demand for ASML's products for longer. To sum up, I think that ASML is a compelling investment opportunity at the moment, deserving a Strong Buy rating.

Reasons To Invest In ASML Before Q3 Earnings

Since it is my first ASML coverage, let's briefly discuss the business and strategic positioning.

ASML Holding N.V. is one of the largest European companies, with a market capitalization of close to $400 billion. It is a Netherlands-based company that provides advanced semiconductor capital equipment. The company boasts monopolistic positioning in manufacturing extreme ultraviolet lithography systems, which are vital for the production of integrated circuits.

Having unmatched strategic positioning in such a vital domain experiencing secular digitalization tailwinds means that ASML is able to exercise extremely strong pricing power, which helps to drive revenue growth and profitability expansion. As we can see, ASML's levered free cash flow average growth rate over the last decade was much higher than revenue growth. To me, it means very strong operating leverage.

The company enjoys strong revenue growth momentum thanks to the general boom in the broader semiconductor environment due to multiple secular tailwinds, where the AI revolution is the biggest one. Thus, ASML maintained a 20%+ YoY revenue growth rate over the last three quarters.

The company releases its Q3 2025 earnings on October 15, and revenue is expected to maintain a double-digit growth rate both in Q3 and Q4. This underscores that growth momentum remains strong, even taking into account sharply increased comparative numbers. The non-GAAP EPS is also expected to show growth YoY, from $5.75 to $6.19. Therefore, ASML's robust operating leverage is still certain.

The management is usually quite conservative in their forecasts, which helps to maintain a strong earnings surprise record. There were no single EPS miss over the last 16 quarters, which means that there will be no bad surprises during the Q3 2025 earnings season.

Apart from a solid record of earnings surprises, there are a few other strong reasons to be optimistic before the upcoming earnings release. ASML's momentum is accelerating, which is always good during earnings seasons, as it is a source of optimism from the market, which can be multiplied by the positive earnings surprise. The stock demonstrated weakness during the first seven months of 2025 due to investors' fears related to trade wars between Europe and the U.S. However, once trade tensions started easing after the trade deal agreed between the parties, ASML started accumulating momentum. As a result, the stock rose from sub-$700 in early August to around $1,000 per share at the moment.

Recent developments in the industry are also positive before the upcoming earnings release. The AI revolution maintains its aggressive momentum, which I see from the fact that global semiconductor sales surged by almost 22% YoY in August. Furthermore, ASML's crucial customer and the world's largest semiconductor fab, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM), also plans to release its Q3 earnings next week. Analysts are very optimistic about TSM's potential to deliver blowout Q3 earnings, citing strong AI chip demand. Therefore, it is no surprise that analysts from Morgan Stanley expect strong demand for wafer fab equipment in 2026 following a record-setting 2025. Therefore, ASML's management's comments regarding the longer-term outlook are also to be strong and optimistic.

With such a positive environment, I also expect the management to share their ambitious development and innovation plans. The company's R&D budget has been growing quite aggressively over the last few years, increasing the chances of new technological insights from the management. I also expect the management to shed light on strategic long-term plans related to the recent large investment in Mistral AI. This was a very interesting move because ASML mostly focuses on hardware, while Mistral AI is a software company. Therefore, with this acquisition, ASML demonstrates its plans to diversify its business mix, which looks promising.

Last but not least, capital allocation is exemplary. ASML's tangible book value per share soared from $26 to $38 over the last five quarters, which is a huge positive sign, meaning that shareholders' wealth is accumulating rapidly. Quite low leverage and a stable outstanding share count also underscore the management's prudent and disciplined capital allocation approach. Therefore, the company is well-prepared not only to weather temporary unexpected setbacks but also to continue investing aggressively in expanding its technological moat and diversifying the business mix.

Prudent capital allocation also supports ASML's stellar dividend growth. Yes, the current yield of 0.72% is extremely low. However, the last five years' average dividend growth rate is 22%. The annual TTM payout is $7.15, meaning that investors who bought the stock around ten years ago, when it traded below $100, currently earn a hefty dividend yield. Therefore, investors are likely to benefit not only from the expected further share price appreciation, but from dividend growth as well.

Overall, I think there are a lot of reasons to be optimistic before the upcoming Q3 earnings release.

Reasons To Avoid ASML

The number of cases when the largest tech entrepreneurs use the "AI bubble" wording publicly. Sam Altman spoke about the AI bubble in August, and so did Jeff Bezos just a few days ago. Indeed, several of the largest tech players have already cumulatively invested hundreds of billions in AI infrastructure, and payback periods for these vast investments are still quite uncertain. If there really is a so-called "AI bubble" and it bursts, valuations of all semiconductor stocks will inevitably suffer.

Additionally, even if ASML delivers blowout Q3 earnings, the post-earnings share price movement is always unpredictable. Furthermore, the market's reaction might be muted even with strong earnings because ASML's share price is very close to a big psychological level of $1,000. Even a strong earnings release might not be enough to break through this milestone.

The Verdict

I think there are significantly more reasons to invest in ASML before the Q3 earnings release compared to reasons to avoid the stock. Moreover, valuation is compelling, meaning that investors can buy rock-solid fundamentals at a quite cheap price. Therefore, my rating for ASML is Strong Buy.
